gaps.description: The Bill of Rights (BoR) is a scaffold without any substantive articles (Scope of Enterprise, Prohibited Activities, Core Identity, etc.). The MSP is strictly subordinate to the BoR and Nomos/Arbiter require a complete BoR for scope verification. Without a defined BoR, the DIO lacks its constitutional identity and the governance system cannot enforce boundaries or prohibited activities.

The MSP contains no operational rules. It is an empty seed, and Nomos (#200) evaluates Action Proposals against MSP constraints. With no rules, Nomos cannot enforce any safety, scope, or operational constraints beyond the BoR scope gate (itself non-functional due to BoR scaffold). The DIO operates in a default-permissive mode, making it vulnerable to unconstrained autonomous actions.

No genome documents exist under genome/. The MSP genome references are intended to extend organ-specific operating rules and risk-tier constraints. While currently the MSP lacks rules, genome documents will be needed to implement organ-level safety constraints once rules are defined. Their absence delays any granular risk management.

raw_responses:
{
  "gaps": [
    {
      "id": "gap-bor-001",
      "title": "Bill of Rights entirely undefined — no constitutional perimeter for memory/context formation",
      "description": "The BoR (Bill of Rights) document is a scaffold awaiting human authorship. All articles (Scope of Enterprise, Prohibited Activities, Core Identity, Human Authority Reservation, Amendment Protocol, Interpretation Rules) are empty. As the MSP is explicitly subordinate to the BoR, the absence of any BoR content means there is no higher-level authority defining what the DIO is supposed to do, what boundaries it must respect, or what identity it should preserve. For memory and context formation, this means the system has no strategic definition of what knowledge is in-scope, what must be remembered, or what constitutes mission-critical state. Every operational memory or context block stored may be strategically irrelevant or even harmful. This is the foundational gap that makes all other strategic gaps unsolvable.",
      "grounding": "BoR Article I — Scope of Enterprise (undefined), Article II — Prohibited Activities (undefined), Article III — Core Identity (undefined)",
      "criticality": 5,
      "urgency": 4,
      "impact": 5,
      "priority_score": 100
    },
    {
      "id": "gap-dream-health-001",
      "title": "Dream-cycle autonomous operation is disabled and has never been validated",
      "description": "The dream-cycle stats show: scheduled: disabled, health: never_run, last_dream: null. The Phase 1 cron-based script (scr-bash-radiant-dreamer-phase1.sh) exists but the cycle overall is not running autonomously. The MSP’s purpose is to translate the BoR into machine-actionable objectives that maintain a persistent state machine; memory consolidation and context freshness are central to that objective. Without a scheduled dream cycle, the DIO’s memory store will grow stale, duplicate entries will accumulate (as evidenced by clusters of nearly identical backup/Git failure entries), and Phase 2 consolidation (LLM-driven merging) will remain deferred indefinitely. This directly undermines the DIO’s ability to maintain an accurate, reliable internal state—a failure to meet the operational constitution intent of the MSP.",
      "grounding": "MSP Purpose (translates BoR into machine-actionable objectives, sustains operational integrity of the DIO as a persistent state machine); MSP Rules (currently empty, no rule requiring periodic memory maintenance)",
      "criticality": 4,
      "urgency": 5,
      "impact": 4,
      "priority_score": 80
    },
    {
      "id": "gap-memory-consolidation-001",
      "title": "Memory consolidation (Phase 2) is repeatedly deferred, leading to fragmentation and redundant entries",
      "description": "Multiple dream cycle reports (e.g., #12, #13, #15) note that Phase 2 (LLM-driven memory consolidation) was deferred to the next interactive session because it requires an LLM. In one case (Cycle #14), consolidation was performed via direct psql with limited capability, leaving semantic similarity scanning undone. The net result is that clusters of similar entries (e.g., recurring backup failures with identical metadata, push-src duplicate histories) persist across cycles without being merged into consolidated memory objects. The MSP’s function is to maintain a clean, machine-actionable state; fragmented and duplicative memory hinders downstream organ functions (Radiant’s relevance judgments, Minder’s deduction/induction, and the DIO’s overall coherence). The absence of MSP rules governing consolidation timelines or quality thresholds creates a structural gap where memory health drifts without automated guardrails.",
      "grounding": "MSP Purpose (machine-actionable state must be accurate and non-redundant); MSP Rules (no rules exist to mandate memory consolidation frequency, quality, or fallback behavior when LLM is unavailable)",
      "criticality": 3,
      "urgency": 4,
      "impact": 3,
      "priority_score": 36
    },
    {
      "id": "gap-strategic-priority-001",
      "title": "No strategic prioritisation rules for memory/context formation — storage is undifferentiated and may miss critical signals",
      "description": "The current context/memory system stores events nearly indiscriminately (backup failures, git sync errors, session completions). There is no indication of strategic filters that prioritise context blocks based on their relevance to the DIO’s mission (which is itself undefined in the BoR). The MSP includes no rules about what constitutes high-value versus low-value context, no validation that stored memories align with BoR objectives, and no mechanisms for associating memories with strategic OKRs. This leads to context pollution (redundant backup error entries dominate recent context), potential performance degradation for LLM-based organs, and, most importantly, a strategic blind spot where the DIO cannot distinguish signal from noise in its own memory. Without a BoR-defined mission, the MSP cannot enforce priority, resulting in a drift toward operational trivia rather than long-term strategic intelligence.",
      "grounding": "BoR Article I — Scope of Enterprise (undefined, so no criteria for what is strategically relevant); MSP Purpose (needs to translate BoR objectives into actionable memory/context rules); MSP Rules (none to define strategic priorities for knowledge capture)",
      "criticality": 3,
      "urgency": 3,
      "impact": 4,
      "priority_score": 36
    }
  ]
}
